Name: multi_scale_spectral_inference
Title: Multi-Scale Spectral Stability-Aware Inference for Efficient Latency-Bounded Computation
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic adjustment of computation scale (e.g., layer depth, attention granularity) based on spectral stability metrics derived from activation geometry can enable efficient inference under strict latency budgets without retraining.
Related Work: Related works include Spectral Quantization Routing (training-free precision allocation) and Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation (latency-bounded ICL). This proposal distinguishes by explicitly linking spectral stability to dynamic multi-scale computation, enabling adaptive layer depth and attention granularity based on spectral diagnostics.
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) face significant challenges in achieving efficient inference under strict latency budgets, especially in resource-constrained environments. Existing approaches often rely on static computation strategies or costly retraining, limiting their adaptability to dynamic input distributions. We propose a training-free, multi-scale spectral stability-aware inference framework that dynamically adjusts computation scale (e.g., layer depth, attention granularity) based on spectral stability metrics derived from activation geometry. By computing spectral diagnostics (e.g., condition number, spectral decay) on-the-fly, our framework identifies stable subspaces suitable for reduced computation while maintaining accuracy. We theoretically ground our approach in spectral stability bounds, showing that low-rank, well-conditioned subspaces can guide efficient computation scaling. Experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our framework achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static baselines, enforcing strict latency budgets without retraining. This work bridges spectral theory and practical inference optimization, offering a reproducible, zero-training path to efficient multi-scale computation.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate spectral stability metrics (e.g., condition number, spectral decay) across layers and attention heads for diverse inputs. Metrics: Spectral diagnostics, perturbation sensitivity.
Experiment 2: Implement dynamic computation scaling based on spectral stability. Compare static vs. dynamic scaling strategies. Metrics: Accuracy, latency, FLOPs.
Experiment 3: Test framework on adversarial and out-of-distribution benchmarks. Metrics: Robustness, throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier.
Experiment 4: Validate latency bounds under varying spectral stability thresholds. Metrics: Latency variance, computational efficiency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Spectral diagnostics may introduce computational overhead, offsetting latency gains. Mitigation: Optimize diagnostics via randomized SVD.
Risk 2: Dynamic scaling may lead to instability in highly noisy inputs. Mitigation: Introduce stability thresholds and fallback mechanisms.
Limitation 1: Framework assumes well-conditioned activation subspaces, which may not hold for all inputs.
Limitation 2: Theoretical bounds rely on idealized assumptions, requiring empirical validation.
